sections.title: Payment in respect of periods of leave
sections.summary: Regulation 16 provides the right to be PAID during annual leave. A worker is entitled to a WEEK'S PAY for each week of leave, calculated by reference to ss.221–224 ERA 1996. The critical distinction is between Reg.13 leave (4 weeks, EU-derived — paid at NORMAL REMUNERATION including regular overtime, commission, and allowances) and Reg.13A leave (1.6 weeks, domestic — paid at BASIC pay). The reference period for calculating a week's pay is 52 weeks (changed from 12 weeks by SI 2020/356 from 6 April 2020). For IRREGULAR HOURS and PART-YEAR workers: from leave years beginning on or after 1 April 2024, an entirely new regime applies (Reg.15B accrual at 12.07% of hours worked per pay period; optional Reg.16A rolled-up holiday pay at 12.07% of total earnings). This reversed Harpur Trust v Brazel [2022] UKSC for these categories of worker.
sections.text:
16.—(1) A worker is entitled to be paid in respect of any period of annual leave to which he is entitled under regulation 13, at the rate of a week's pay in respect of each week of leave.

(3) A week's pay—
(a) is calculated in accordance with sections 221 to 224 of the 1996 Act; and
(b) for the purposes of that calculation, the calculation date is the first day of the period of leave in question.

(3A) In determining the amount of a week's pay of a worker for the purposes of this regulation, any payment made to the worker by the employer in a relevant period shall be treated as being remuneration payable in respect of the period of time worked by the worker in that period.
sections.keyPrinciple: Regulation 16 is the PAYMENT obligation for annual leave. The right to leave itself is in Reg.13 (4 weeks EU-derived) and Reg.13A (1.6 weeks domestic). Reg.16 answers: HOW MUCH must the worker be paid? The answer depends on the type of leave and the type of worker. For standard workers: Reg.13 leave must be paid at NORMAL REMUNERATION (which includes regular overtime, regular commission, regular allowances — as established by CJEU and UK case law). Reg.13A leave is paid at the BASIC rate only. For irregular hours and part-year workers (from leave years starting 1 April 2024): the entire framework is replaced by Reg.15B (accrual) and Reg.16A (optional rolled-up holiday pay).

reg13Leave.derivation: EU-derived — implements Article 7 of the Working Time Directive 2003/88/EC
reg13Leave.payRate: NORMAL REMUNERATION — a week's pay calculated under ss.221–224 ERA 1996 using a 52-week reference period
normalRemunerationIncludes: Regular overtime (Bear Scotland Ltd v Fulton [2015] EAT)
Results-based commission (British Gas Trading Ltd v Lock [2017] EWCA)
Regular allowances and supplements paid during normal working hours
Regular shift premiums and unsocial hours payments
normalRemunerationExcludes: Purely voluntary overtime that is irregular and not expected
Discretionary bonuses (unless they form part of normal remuneration)
reg13Leave.carryOver: Reg.13 leave can be carried over where: (a) the worker was unable to take it due to sickness (codified from NHS Leeds v Larner); (b) the worker was on maternity/family leave; (c) the employer failed to enable the worker to take it or inform them of the right

reg13ALeave.derivation: Domestic — UK-specific additional leave (introduced by Working Time (Amendment) Regulations 2007)
reg13ALeave.payRate: BASIC pay — does not include regular overtime, commission, or allowances
reg13ALeave.carryOver: Reg.13A leave can be carried over by agreement between the parties or where the employer failed to enable the worker to take it

twoTierSystem.practicalNote: The two-tier system creates complexity: the first 4 weeks of leave must be paid at the higher rate (normal remuneration), the remaining 1.6 weeks at basic pay. Most employers apply the higher rate to ALL leave to simplify administration — this satisfies the legal obligation but increases cost.

referenceperiod.current: 52 weeks (changed from 12 weeks by the Employment Rights (Employment Particulars and Paid Annual Leave) (Amendment) Regulations 2018 (SI 2018/1378) — in force from 6 April 2020)
referenceperiod.howItWorks: To calculate a week's pay, look back over the 52 weeks BEFORE the calculation date (the first day of the leave period). Exclude any weeks in which no remuneration was payable. If there are excluded weeks, extend the reference period further back until 52 weeks with remuneration are found (up to a maximum of 104 weeks).
referenceperiod.significance: The extension from 12 to 52 weeks smooths out seasonal variations and reduces the risk of manipulation by timing leave to fall after low-pay periods.

definitions.irregularHoursWorker: A worker for whom the number of paid hours they will work within a pay period is, under the terms of their contract, WHOLLY OR MOSTLY VARIABLE (Reg.15F(1)(a)). Captures zero-hours workers and those with genuinely variable hours.
definitions.partYearWorker: A worker for whom, in a given leave year and under the terms of their contract, there are periods of at least a WEEK in which they are not required to work and are not paid (Reg.15F(1)(b)). Captures term-time only workers, seasonal workers.

newRegime.instrument: Employment Rights (Amendment, Revocation and Transitional Provision) Regulations 2023 (SI 2023/1086)
newRegime.effectiveDate: Leave years beginning on or after 1 April 2024
newRegime.accrual: Reg.15B — holiday entitlement accrues at 12.07% of hours worked in each pay period. No distinction between Reg.13 and Reg.13A — a SINGLE unified entitlement of 5.6 weeks.
newRegime.rolledUpHolidayPay: Reg.16A — employers may choose to pay rolled-up holiday pay for irregular hours and part-year workers. Calculated as 12.07% of total earnings within the pay period. Must be paid with each payslip and clearly marked as a separate item. The worker cannot request rolled-up holiday pay — it is the EMPLOYER'S choice.
newRegime.reversalOfBrazel: This regime deliberately reverses Harpur Trust v Brazel [2022] UKSC by making the 12.07% accrual method the statutory default for irregular/part-year workers. The Supreme Court had held this method was unlawful — the 2023 Regulations make it lawful again for these categories.

irregularAndPartYearWorkers.notApplicableTo: Standard workers (fixed hours, working throughout the year) remain subject to the traditional Reg.13/13A/16 framework. The Reg.15B/16A regime applies ONLY to irregular hours and part-year workers.

harpurTrustVBrazel.facts: Ms Brazel was a part-time music teacher on a zero-hours contract. She worked during term-time only (32–35 weeks per year). The school calculated her holiday pay using the 12.07% method — paying 12.07% of hours worked per term.
harpurTrustVBrazel.held: The Supreme Court UNANIMOUSLY held that part-year workers are entitled to 5.6 weeks of annual leave calculated using the calendar week method (ss.221–224 ERA 1996) WITHOUT pro-rating. The 12.07% method was UNLAWFUL because it pro-rated the entitlement — something the WTR did not permit.
harpurTrustVBrazel.currentStatus: REVERSED for irregular hours and part-year workers by SI 2023/1086 (from leave years starting 1 April 2024). STILL GOOD LAW for all other workers — the calendar week method remains the default for standard workers.

versions.text: [Reg.16 amended by SI 2023/1086 to include Reg.16(3A) (payments treated as remuneration for 'normal remuneration' calculation). New Reg.15B (accrual for irregular/part-year workers) and Reg.16A (rolled-up holiday pay) inserted. 52-week reference period from 6 April 2020 (SI 2018/1378).]
versions.amendedBy: WTR 1998 as enacted; amended by SI 2007/2079 (Reg.13A), SI 2018/1378 (52-week reference period), SI 2023/1086 (irregular/part-year regime, rolled-up holiday pay, carry-over codification)

pendingAmendment.effect: ERA 2025 does not directly amend Reg.16. However, the Government has indicated it is contemplating more fundamental reforms to the rate of holiday pay — potentially merging the Reg.13/Reg.13A distinction into a single entitlement paid at normal remuneration. No consultation has been published. Separately, ERA 2025 extends tribunal time limits from 3 months to 6 months (from October 2026), which affects holiday pay claims under Reg.30.
pendingAmendment.aceiImpact: MODERATE — the potential merger of Reg.13/Reg.13A into a single rate would simplify calculation but increase costs for employers who currently pay Reg.13A leave at basic rate.

refs: WTR 1998 Reg.13 (entitlement to 4 weeks EU-derived annual leave)
WTR 1998 Reg.13A (entitlement to 1.6 weeks domestic annual leave)
WTR 1998 Reg.14 (payment on termination — accrued but untaken leave)
WTR 1998 Reg.15B (accrual for irregular hours and part-year workers — 12.07%)
WTR 1998 Reg.16 (this regulation — payment during leave)
WTR 1998 Reg.16A (rolled-up holiday pay for irregular/part-year workers)
WTR 1998 Reg.15F (definitions of irregular hours and part-year workers)
WTR 1998 Reg.30 (complaint to tribunal — time limit 3 months, extending to 6 months Oct 2026)
ERA 1996 ss.221–224 (calculation of a week's pay — reference period, normal working hours)
ERA 1996 s.227 (maximum week's pay cap — applies to statutory redundancy/basic award but NOT to holiday pay)
Working Time Directive 2003/88/EC Art.7 (EU minimum 4 weeks paid leave — no longer directly applicable post-Brexit but retained in Reg.13)
SI 2023/1086 (Employment Rights (Amendment, Revocation and Transitional Provision) Regulations 2023 — the Brazel reversal)

leadingCases.name: Harpur Trust v Brazel
leadingCases.principle: Part-year workers are entitled to 5.6 weeks of annual leave calculated using the calendar week method (ss.221–224 ERA 1996) WITHOUT pro-rating. The 12.07% method was unlawful. REVERSED for irregular/part-year workers by SI 2023/1086 (from leave years starting 1 April 2024). STILL GOOD LAW for standard workers.
leadingCases.heldText: The Supreme Court unanimously held that part-year workers must receive 5.6 weeks' leave paid at a week's pay calculated under the ERA 1996. The WTR did not permit pro-rating the entitlement by reference to weeks actually worked. The 12.07% method was unlawful because it applied a pro-rating formula that had no statutory basis.
leadingCases.significance: THE landmark UKSC authority on holiday pay for part-year workers. Triggered the legislative reversal via SI 2023/1086. Now reversed for irregular/part-year workers but remains good law for the proposition that standard workers' holiday cannot be pro-rated.

leadingCases.name: Bear Scotland Ltd v Fulton
leadingCases.principle: Regular overtime must be included in the calculation of holiday pay for Reg.13 leave (EU-derived leave). Overtime that is sufficiently regular and settled (including non-guaranteed overtime that the worker is required to work if offered) forms part of 'normal remuneration'. Also established the 3-month gap rule for series of deductions claims under ERA 1996 s.23.
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that non-guaranteed overtime that was worked regularly and sufficiently frequently formed part of the worker's normal remuneration and must be included in Reg.13 holiday pay calculations. Also held that a gap of more than 3 months between deductions breaks the 'series of deductions' for s.23 claims.
leadingCases.significance: THE foundational EAT authority on the inclusion of regular overtime in holiday pay AND the 3-month gap rule for backdating claims.

leadingCases.name: British Gas Trading Ltd v Lock
leadingCases.principle: Results-based commission that is intrinsically linked to the performance of the worker's tasks must be included in the calculation of Reg.13 holiday pay. If the worker normally receives commission as part of their regular remuneration, excluding it from holiday pay would deter the worker from taking leave — contrary to the purpose of the Working Time Directive.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that commission payments intrinsically linked to the worker's contractual duties formed part of normal remuneration and must be included in Reg.13 holiday pay calculations.
leadingCases.significance: Extends the 'normal remuneration' principle to results-based commission — not just basic pay + overtime.

leadingCases.name: Robinson-Steele v RD Retail Services Ltd
leadingCases.principle: Rolled-up holiday pay (paying an enhancement in lieu of holiday pay) was UNLAWFUL under the Working Time Directive because it discourages workers from taking leave. However, amounts paid as rolled-up holiday pay could be offset against the worker's entitlement. NOW REVERSED for irregular/part-year workers by Reg.16A (from leave years starting 1 April 2024) — rolled-up holiday pay is lawful for these categories if properly administered.
leadingCases.heldText: The CJEU held that the practice of 'rolling up' holiday pay into basic pay was incompatible with Article 7 of the Working Time Directive. Workers must receive actual payment during leave, not an enhancement paid throughout the year.
leadingCases.significance: THE CJEU authority that made rolled-up holiday pay unlawful — now partially reversed by domestic legislation for irregular/part-year workers. Remains relevant for standard workers (rolled-up holiday pay is still not expressly authorised for them).

leadingCases.name: NHS Leeds v Larner
leadingCases.principle: A worker who is unable to take Reg.13 leave due to sickness is entitled to carry it over to the following leave year. The right to carry over exists regardless of whether the employer's policy permits carry-over. This right has been codified in the 2023 Regulations.
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that a worker on long-term sick leave was entitled to carry over untaken Reg.13 leave because she was unable to exercise the right due to sickness.
leadingCases.significance: Established the carry-over right for sick workers — now codified in the WTR by SI 2023/1086. The codification puts carry-over rights on a statutory footing rather than relying on CJEU case law.

leadingCases.name: Agnew v Commissioner for His Majesty's Revenue and Customs
leadingCases.principle: The correct approach to calculating holiday pay for Reg.13 leave is to look at what the worker NORMALLY receives — including all elements of remuneration that are 'normally' paid. The test is whether the payments are sufficiently regular and settled to constitute normal remuneration. The Supreme Court confirmed the broad approach to normal remuneration and held that the distinction between Reg.13 and Reg.13A leave means that Reg.13 leave must be paid at the full normal remuneration rate.
leadingCases.heldText: The Supreme Court held that holiday pay for Reg.13 leave must reflect what the worker would normally earn, including overtime and other regular payments. The Reg.13/Reg.13A distinction was upheld — Reg.13A leave can be paid at basic rate.
leadingCases.significance: THE most recent UKSC authority on holiday pay calculation. Confirms the broad 'normal remuneration' approach for Reg.13 leave and upholds the two-tier Reg.13/Reg.13A distinction.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Holiday pay and entitlement reforms from 1 January 2024
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on the 2023/2024 holiday pay reforms including the 12.07% accrual method, rolled-up holiday pay, and carry-over codification.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Checking holiday entitlement
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on calculating holiday entitlement and pay for different types of worker.

commonErrors: Not understanding the two-tier system — Reg.13 leave (4 weeks) must be paid at NORMAL REMUNERATION (including regular overtime, commission, allowances). Reg.13A leave (1.6 weeks) can be paid at BASIC rate. Many employers incorrectly pay all 5.6 weeks at basic rate — this underpays Reg.13 leave. The two-tier distinction was upheld by the Supreme Court in Agnew v HMRC [2023]

Not including regular overtime in Reg.13 holiday pay — non-guaranteed overtime that is worked regularly and sufficiently frequently must be included in the calculation of Reg.13 holiday pay (Bear Scotland v Fulton [2015]). The overtime must be 'sufficiently regular and settled' — genuinely occasional or voluntary overtime may be excluded, but overtime that is routinely worked as part of the normal pattern must be included

Not including commission in Reg.13 holiday pay — results-based commission intrinsically linked to the worker's contractual duties must be included in Reg.13 holiday pay (British Gas v Lock [2017] EWCA). Excluding commission discourages workers from taking leave — which defeats the purpose of the holiday entitlement

Still using the 12-week reference period — the reference period was extended from 12 weeks to 52 WEEKS from 6 April 2020 (SI 2018/1378). Employers still calculating on a 12-week basis are using the wrong reference period. The 52-week lookback excludes weeks with no remuneration and extends further back (up to 104 weeks) to find 52 paid weeks

Applying the Brazel calculation to irregular/part-year workers after 1 April 2024 — Harpur Trust v Brazel [2022] UKSC was REVERSED for irregular hours and part-year workers by SI 2023/1086. From leave years starting 1 April 2024, these workers accrue holiday at 12.07% of hours worked per pay period (Reg.15B). Continuing to apply Brazel after this date means NOT using the statutory method. However, Brazel remains good law for STANDARD workers

Using rolled-up holiday pay for standard workers — Reg.16A permits rolled-up holiday pay ONLY for irregular hours and part-year workers. Standard workers (fixed hours, working year-round) are NOT eligible for rolled-up holiday pay. For them, Robinson-Steele v RD Retail [2006] CJEU still applies — holiday pay must be paid when leave is taken, not rolled into regular pay

Not correctly identifying irregular hours and part-year workers — Reg.15F defines: (a) irregular hours worker = hours are WHOLLY OR MOSTLY VARIABLE; (b) part-year worker = there are periods of at least a WEEK with no work and no pay. Misclassification means applying the wrong calculation method. Workers with slightly variable hours but a broadly fixed pattern may NOT qualify as irregular hours workers

Not marking rolled-up holiday pay separately on payslips — Reg.16A requires rolled-up holiday pay to be paid with each payslip and clearly marked as a SEPARATE ITEM. An employer who rolls up holiday pay but does not separately identify it on the payslip fails to comply with the transparency requirement

Not understanding carry-over rights — the 2023 Regulations codified carry-over rights for: (a) workers unable to take leave due to sickness (NHS Leeds v Larner); (b) workers on maternity/family leave; (c) workers whose employer failed to enable them to take leave or inform them of the right. These carry-over rights apply regardless of the employer's contractual policy. Untaken Reg.13 leave carried over on sickness grounds must be taken within 18 months of the end of the leave year in which it accrued

Assuming the s.227 weekly pay cap applies to holiday pay — the s.227 cap on a week's pay (£751 from April 2026) applies to statutory redundancy payments and the basic award for unfair dismissal. It does NOT apply to holiday pay under Reg.16. Holiday pay is calculated on the worker's ACTUAL average weekly remuneration with NO cap. A worker earning £2,000 per week receives £2,000 per week of Reg.13 holiday pay

Not understanding the interaction between holiday pay and wages deduction claims — underpayment of holiday pay is an unlawful deduction from wages under ERA 1996 s.13, claimable under s.23. The 'series of deductions' rule applies — the worker can claim all underpayments in an unbroken series (Bear Scotland gap rule: 3-month break ends the series). Combined with the extended 6-month time limit from October 2026, this creates significant back-pay exposure for employers who have systematically underpaid holiday
